Clonal Tracking & AUC Kinetics

TCR Repertoire Analysis — Notebook 05

Joshua Luthy

2026-03-15

Analysis Overview

This notebook tracks individual clonotype kinetics across longitudinal timepoints following cell therapy infusion. We follow clonotypes from the manufactured Product through post-infusion peripheral blood samples at Weeks 2, 4, 8, and 12 to characterize:

  • Expansion kinetics — how quickly do Product clones expand in vivo?
  • Peak timing — when do clones reach maximum frequency?
  • Persistence — do expanded clones persist or decline?
  • AUC (area under the curve) — time-integrated clonal exposure as a summary metric
longitudinal <- read_csv("../data/processed/longitudinal_clonal_tracking.csv")
auc_metrics  <- read_csv("../data/processed/clonal_auc_metrics.csv")
patient_meta <- read_csv("../data/raw/patient_metadata.csv")

# Define timepoint ordering
tp_order <- c("Apheresis", "Product", "Wk2", "Wk4", "Wk8", "Wk12")
longitudinal <- longitudinal %>%
  mutate(timepoint = factor(timepoint, levels = tp_order),
         clinical_response = factor(clinical_response, levels = c("CR", "PR", "PD")))

auc_metrics <- auc_metrics %>%
  mutate(clinical_response = factor(clinical_response, levels = c("CR", "PR", "PD")))
6
Timepoints
300
Tracked Clonotypes
120
Product Clones Tracked
12 wks
Follow-up Duration

Longitudinal Clonal Trajectories

Top 5 Clonotypes per Patient

We plot the frequency trajectory of the top 5 Product clonotypes across all timepoints for each patient.

top5_tracks <- longitudinal %>%
  filter(is_product_clone, clone_rank_in_product <= 5)

# Numeric x position for plotting
tp_weeks <- c("Apheresis" = -2, "Product" = 0, "Wk2" = 2, "Wk4" = 4, "Wk8" = 8, "Wk12" = 12)
top5_tracks <- top5_tracks %>%
  mutate(week = tp_weeks[as.character(timepoint)])

ggplot(top5_tracks, aes(x = week, y = clone_fraction, 
                         group = cdr3_aa, color = factor(clone_rank_in_product))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("1" = "#00d4ff", "2" = "#7b2fff", "3" = "#00ff9d", 
               "4" = "#ffd93d", "5" = "#ff6b6b"),
    labels = paste("Clone", 1:5)
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 8, 12),
    labels = c("Aph", "Prod", "Wk2", "Wk4", "Wk8", "Wk12")
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ patient_id, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    x = "Timepoint", y = "Clone Fraction",
    color = "Product Rank",
    title = "Top 5 Product Clonotype Trajectories",
    subtitle = "Frequency tracked from Apheresis through 12 weeks post-infusion"
  ) +
  theme(
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "#152231", color = "#1e3348"),
    strip.text = element_text(color = "#00d4ff", face = "bold", size = 11),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

Observation: CR patients show dramatic post-infusion expansion of their top Product clones, with frequencies peaking at Wk4–Wk8 and sustained persistence through Wk12. PD patients show weaker, more transient expansion with rapid decline.

CR vs PD Kinetics Comparison

# Average trajectory of top clone by response
top1_avg <- longitudinal %>%
  filter(is_product_clone, clone_rank_in_product == 1) %>%
  mutate(week = tp_weeks[as.character(timepoint)]) %>%
  group_by(clinical_response, week) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_freq = mean(clone_fraction),
    se_freq   = sd(clone_fraction) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(top1_avg, aes(x = week, y = mean_freq, color = clinical_response,
                      fill = clinical_response)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = mean_freq - se_freq, ymax = mean_freq + se_freq),
              alpha = 0.15, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(-2, 0, 2, 4, 8, 12),
    labels = c("Aph", "Prod", "Wk2", "Wk4", "Wk8", "Wk12")
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Timepoint", y = "Mean Clone Fraction (Top Clone)",
    color = "Response", fill = "Response",
    title = "Top Clone Kinetics by Clinical Response",
    subtitle = "Mean ± SE of the dominant Product clone — CR patients show strongest, most sustained expansion"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Area Under the Curve (AUC) Analysis

AUC provides a single summary metric for time-integrated clonal exposure — capturing both the magnitude and duration of expansion. We compute AUC using the trapezoidal rule over the post-infusion timepoints (Wk2 through Wk12).

# Show the AUC formula approach
cat("AUC computed via trapezoidal integration:\n")
## AUC computed via trapezoidal integration:
cat("  AUC = Σ 0.5 × (f(t_i) + f(t_{i+1})) × Δt\n")
##   AUC = Σ 0.5 × (f(t_i) + f(t_{i+1})) × Δt
cat("  Over timepoints: Wk2, Wk4, Wk8, Wk12\n")
##   Over timepoints: Wk2, Wk4, Wk8, Wk12
cat("  Units: frequency × weeks\n")
##   Units: frequency × weeks
auc_metrics %>%
  group_by(patient_id, clinical_response) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_auc = round(mean(auc), 4),
    median_auc = round(median(auc), 4),
    max_auc = round(max(auc), 4),
    mean_persistence = round(mean(persistence_ratio), 3),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "AUC summary per patient (top 20 Product clonotypes)")
AUC summary per patient (top 20 Product clonotypes)
patient_id clinical_response mean_auc median_auc max_auc mean_persistence
PT-001 CR 2.5144 0.0292 39.5899 0.856
PT-002 CR 1.4826 0.0298 21.4535 0.789
PT-003 PR 0.7044 0.0467 9.4410 0.310
PT-004 PR 0.7002 0.0304 9.6012 0.302
PT-005 PD 0.2287 0.0524 1.9401 0.086
PT-006 PD 0.2544 0.0389 2.8540 0.097

AUC Distribution by Clinical Response

ggplot(auc_metrics, aes(x = clinical_response, y = log10(auc + 0.0001),
                         fill = clinical_response)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.3, outlier.shape = NA, width = 0.5, color = "#3f6380") +
  geom_jitter(aes(color = clinical_response), width = 0.1, size = 2.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  labs(
    x = "Clinical Response", y = expression(log[10] ~ "(AUC)"),
    title = "Clonal AUC by Clinical Response",
    subtitle = "Higher AUC indicates greater time-integrated clonal exposure"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

AUC vs Peak Frequency

ggplot(auc_metrics, aes(x = log10(peak_frequency + 0.0001), y = log10(auc + 0.0001),
                         color = clinical_response)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  labs(
    x = expression(log[10] ~ "(Peak Frequency)"),
    y = expression(log[10] ~ "(AUC)"),
    color = "Response",
    title = "AUC vs Peak Frequency",
    subtitle = "Strong correlation — clones that peak higher also accumulate greater total exposure"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Persistence Analysis

We define persistence ratio as the frequency at Wk12 divided by peak frequency. A ratio of 1.0 means the clone maintained its peak level; lower values indicate decline.

ggplot(auc_metrics, aes(x = clinical_response, y = persistence_ratio,
                         fill = clinical_response)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.3, outlier.shape = NA, width = 0.5, color = "#3f6380") +
  geom_jitter(aes(color = clinical_response), width = 0.1, size = 2.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  labs(
    x = "Clinical Response", y = "Persistence Ratio (Wk12 / Peak)",
    title = "Clonal Persistence by Clinical Response",
    subtitle = "CR patients retain a higher fraction of peak expansion through Wk12"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Finding: CR patients exhibit significantly higher persistence ratios, with many clones maintaining 60–90% of their peak frequency at Week 12. PD patients show rapid post-peak decline, with most clones retaining less than 30% of peak levels.

Peak Timing Distribution

auc_metrics %>%
  mutate(peak_timepoint = factor(peak_timepoint, levels = c("Wk2", "Wk4", "Wk8", "Wk12"))) %>%
  count(clinical_response, peak_timepoint) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = peak_timepoint, y = n, fill = clinical_response)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8, width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("CR" = "#00ff9d", "PR" = "#00d4ff", "PD" = "#ff6b6b")) +
  labs(
    x = "Peak Timepoint", y = "Number of Clonotypes",
    fill = "Response",
    title = "Distribution of Peak Expansion Timing",
    subtitle = "When do Product clonotypes reach maximum frequency post-infusion?"
  )

Summary

Key Findings:

  1. CR patients show the strongest and most sustained clonal expansion post-infusion, with top clones peaking at Wk4–Wk8 and maintaining high frequencies through Wk12.
  2. AUC is significantly higher in CR vs PD patients, confirming that time-integrated clonal exposure associates with clinical benefit.
  3. Persistence ratios stratify by response — CR clones retain 60–90% of peak frequency while PD clones decline to <30%.
  4. Peak timing differs by response group — CR clones peak later (Wk4–Wk8) while PD clones peak earlier and more transiently (Wk2).
  5. AUC and peak frequency are strongly correlated, but persistence adds independent prognostic information.
